user_story: As a subscriber, I want messages I send to the shared Telegram bot to be routed to my personal OpenClaw container so that I receive AI responses in Telegram.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/telegram/webhook/ verifies the X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header via HMAC constant-time compare; returns 403 on mismatch, 503 if secret unconfigured. Sets RLS service-role context for DB access. Claims update_id via ProcessedInboundEvent (get_or_create) to deduplicate Telegram retries — returns 200 'ok' immediately on duplicate. Dispatches /start, callback_query, rate-limit, tenant-lookup, status, and budget checks in order before forwarding. On success forwards the decorated update to the tenant's OpenClaw container FQDN at /telegram-webhook with a 30s (or 120s for reasoning models) timeout, records usage, captures the conversation turn, and returns the container's JSON response. On timeout returns 200 'ok' silently (agent replies async via bot token). Returns 400 on malformed JSON.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API, OpenClaw container, Supabase/Postgres (ProcessedInboundEvent, Tenant/User lookup, UsageRecord), apps/billing/services:check_budget, apps/router/inbound_dedup:claim_inbound_event
inventory_notes: Webhook mode is latent in production — the platform runs single-revision with the long-poll poller instead. Webhook path is covered by all-channels rule and kept current. The container response's JSON is returned verbatim to Telegram as a 200+JSON body, which Telegram executes as a Bot API method call (sendMessage etc.).

user_story: As the system, I want a single long-polling process to receive Telegram updates for all tenants so that single-revision Container Apps deployment avoids 409 webhook conflicts.
expected_behaviour: Management command `python manage.py poll_telegram` starts TelegramPoller.start(), which calls deleteWebhook (drop_pending_updates=False) then enters a long-poll loop using getUpdates with timeout=30s, allowed_updates=[message, callback_query, edited_message]. For each update, sets RLS service-role context, claims update_id via claim_inbound_event (idempotency), then calls _handle_update for routing. Offset is advanced only AFTER successful handling or a logged poison-update, not before, so a DB error (e.g. Supabase pool exhaustion in set_rls_context) doesn't ack-and-drop the update. Catches SIGINT/SIGTERM for graceful stop. Calls close_old_connections() after every poll cycle to avoid pinning a Supavisor pool slot.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (getUpdates, deleteWebhook), Supabase/Postgres (RLS context, DB connections), apps/router/inbound_dedup:claim_inbound_event
inventory_notes: Production mode: poller process runs as a separate Azure Container App revision pinned to single-revision mode. Backoff is exponential up to 60s on errors. TimeoutException during long-poll is normal and loops without backoff.

user_story: As the system, I want each Telegram update_id to be processed exactly once so that Telegram's at-least-once redelivery and poller restart replays don't produce duplicate AI replies.
expected_behaviour: claim_inbound_event(f'tg:{update_id}') performs a get_or_create on ProcessedInboundEvent.event_key (UNIQUE column). Returns True (process) if created, False (skip) if already exists. On IntegrityError (concurrent race) returns False. On any other exception (DB down) returns True — fail-open so real messages are never dropped. A 1% probability prune pass deletes up to 500 rows older than 3 days to prevent table growth. Used in both the webhook and the poller path before any user-visible side effect.
inventory_notes: Retention window is 3 days — comfortably longer than Telegram's redelivery window and poller restart replay horizon. Self-pruning is probabilistic (1%) so no separate cron is needed.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send /start TOKEN to the Telegram bot so that my Telegram account is linked to my NBHD subscriber account.
expected_behaviour: Detected at the top of both the webhook (handle_start_command) and the poller (_handle_update) before any routing. Parses /start <token> from the message text. Extracts telegram_user_id, telegram_chat_id, telegram_username, telegram_first_name from the update. Delegates to apps/tenants/telegram_service:process_start_token which validates the token and links the User row. Returns a JSON sendMessage response on success ('Your account is now linked!') or failure. On success, invalidates the in-memory route cache for chat_id so subsequent messages are routed immediately. No auth required — the token is the credential.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (User model update, Tenant lookup), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage response)
inventory_notes: handle_start_command returns None for non-/start messages; callers check for None before routing. Cache invalidation is in-process only; a multi-process deployment would require a shared cache.

user_story: As the system, I want to rate-limit each Telegram chat_id to 30 messages per minute so that individual users cannot flood the system.
expected_behaviour: is_rate_limited(chat_id) maintains an in-memory deque per chat_id. Drops events older than 60s from the front, then checks if the deque length >= ROUTER_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE (default 30). Returns True (rate limited) before appending the new event. If limit <= 0, always returns False. In webhook: returns HTTP 429 'Too many requests'. In poller: logs warning and returns (no response sent). Rate-limit state is in-process memory only.
inventory_notes: ROUTER_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE setting from Django settings. In-memory only — resets on process restart, not shared across workers.

user_story: As an anonymous Telegram user, I want to receive a sign-up link when I message the bot so that I know how to subscribe.
expected_behaviour: When resolve_tenant_by_chat_id returns None (no User with matching telegram_chat_id or no ACTIVE tenant), both the webhook and poller call send_onboarding_link(chat_id) which builds a sendMessage response pointing to the FRONTEND_URL. Webhook returns it as JsonResponse; poller calls _execute_telegram_response. Message: 'I don't recognize your account yet. Sign up at {frontend_url}...'

user_story: As a subscriber whose container is still provisioning, I want to receive a status message so that I know my assistant is not yet ready.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller check tenant.status in (PENDING, PROVISIONING) after tenant lookup. Returns a localized sendMessage response (error_msg(lang, 'waking_up')) telling the user to try again in a minute. No forwarding to OpenClaw occurs. Lang is taken from tenant.user.language or 'en'.

user_story: As a suspended subscriber (payment lapsed, no active subscription), I want to receive a message explaining my account is paused and pointing to billing so I can reactivate.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller check tenant.status == SUSPENDED and not tenant.is_trial and not bool(tenant.stripe_subscription_id). Returns localized error_msg(lang, 'suspended', billing_url=...) pointing to {FRONTEND_URL}/settings/billing. Suspended tenants who still have a stripe_subscription_id or are on trial are not blocked here (trial quota handling takes precedence).

feature: Budget check and container hibernation on quota exhaustion
user_story: As the system, I want to block messages and hibernate the container when a tenant's monthly budget is exhausted so that costs do not overrun.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller call check_budget(tenant) before waking/forwarding. If budget_reason is non-empty, _hibernate_for_quota(tenant) is called: if container is not already hibernated, calls azure_client.hibernate_container_app(container_id), sets Tenant.hibernated_at=now, and calls send_cost_exhausted_email (idempotent via cost_exhausted_email_sent_at). Then _build_budget_exhausted_message / _send_budget_exhausted is called, returning a localized message. Global budget (reason='global') uses 'budget_unavailable' copy; per-tenant uses 'budget_exhausted_trial' or 'budget_exhausted_paid' with a billing URL. Budget-exempt tenants and tenants with purchased_credit skip hibernation via _handle_openrouter_credit_limit.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Tenant.hibernated_at, estimated_cost_this_month, effective_cost_budget), Azure Container Apps (hibernate_container_app), Mailgun/Django email (send_cost_exhausted_email)

user_story: As a subscriber approaching my monthly budget cap, I want to receive an email warning at 90% usage so that I am not surprised when chat stops.
expected_behaviour: send_cost_approaching_email(tenant) is called from billing reconcile cron or the OR credit-limit path when estimated_cost_this_month crosses 90% of effective_cost_budget. Idempotent: skips if cost_warn_sent_at is already set. Skips if no user.email, tenant is budget_exempt, or tenant is not ACTIVE/SUSPENDED. Renders cost_approaching_subject/body.txt/html templates. On success stamps cost_warn_sent_at. fire_threshold_emails_if_crossed(tenant, before, after) compares pre/post costs and fires the appropriate email only when the threshold is crossed upward in this update.
inventory_notes: Reset monthly via reset_monthly_counters so the chain re-arms each cycle.

feature: Hibernated tenant message buffering and wake
user_story: As a subscriber whose container is idle-hibernated, I want my message to be buffered and the container to be woken so that I receive a reply without having to resend.
expected_behaviour: handle_hibernated_message(tenant, 'telegram', update, msg_text) is called when tenant.hibernated_at is set. Checks for existing undelivered BufferedMessage rows: if the oldest is >5 minutes old, the prior wake stalled — forces a fresh wake and clears hibernated_at defensively. Buffers the current message as a BufferedMessage row. On first-ever queued message: calls wake_hibernated_tenant(tenant), returns ACK_FRESH. On additional message during in-flight wake: returns SILENT. On stall recovery: returns ACK_RECONNECT. Caller maps ACK_FRESH→'hibernation_waking' message, ACK_RECONNECT→'hibernation_reconnecting' message, SILENT→HTTP 200 'ok'. Used only in the webhook path; the poller path goes straight to PendingMessage queue and handles hibernation in the drain task.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (BufferedMessage, Tenant.hibernated_at), Azure Container Apps (wake/activate revision via orchestrator)
inventory_notes: Buffered messages are flushed by deliver_buffered_messages_task (orchestrator/hibernation.py) after wake. The drain-task path (PendingMessage) has its own parallel hibernation-on-404 handling.

user_story: As the system, I want rapid-fire messages from the same Telegram chat to be serialized into the OpenClaw session so that the claude-cli backend does not reject concurrent turns.
expected_behaviour: enqueue_message_for_tenant creates a PendingMessage row (tenant, channel='telegram', channel_user_id=str(chat_id), payload, user_text_excerpt), stamps first_message_at on the tenant's first-ever inbound, and publishes a QStash drain_pending_messages_for_tenant task with retries=3. drain_pending_messages_for_tenant_task uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED with a soft delivery_in_flight_until lease to claim the oldest batch per (tenant, channel, channel_user_id) key. Singles and coalesced cold-start batches (voice always singleton) are POST'd to the container as one /v1/chat/completions turn. On success, marks rows DELIVERED, clears stale hibernated_at if live gateway response received. On hibernated 404, calls wake_hibernated_tenant and defers drain by _WAKE_DEFER_SECONDS=20s without burning attempt counter. On boot-grace 404 (within 240s of last_wake_at), releases lease and defers without burning attempt counter. On failure increments delivery_attempts; at _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3 marks FAILED and sends apology. Stale rows older than 600s are dropped with apology instead of delivered.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (PendingMessage model, router migrations 0004/0009), QStash (drain task publish/retry), OpenClaw container (/v1/chat/completions), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage for apology/relay), apps/billing/services:record_usage
inventory_notes: The reaper task (reap_stuck_inbound_messages_task) runs every ~60s via QStash cron and republishes drain tasks for rows stuck PENDING >90s with no live lease — covers publish failures, DLQ, and worker-kill scenarios. Coalesced batches use format_coalesced_user_content to build a single prompt from multiple user_text rows.

feature: Telegram message forwarding to OpenClaw (poller path)
user_story: As a subscriber, I want messages I send on Telegram to be forwarded to my OpenClaw container and the AI response to be delivered back to me.
expected_behaviour: _forward_to_container in the poller decorates message_text with proactive_context block + datetime marker + chat context marker, then calls enqueue_message_for_tenant (PendingMessage queue). The queue drain task POSTs to https://{container_fqdn}/v1/chat/completions with Authorization: Bearer {gateway_token}, X-Telegram-Chat-Id, X-Channel: telegram, X-User-Timezone headers. On a healthy response, relay_ai_response_to_telegram rehydrates PII, strips/records insights markers, renders [[chart:]] markers into images uploaded to Azure File Share, sends MEDIA: images via sendPhoto, and delivers the markdown response via _send_telegram_markdown (render_telegram_html → parse_mode=HTML with plain-text fallback on 400). Returns True on success. Records token usage via _record_usage_safe.
dependencies: OpenClaw container (/v1/chat/completions), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage parse_mode=HTML, sendPhoto), Azure File Share (chart PNG upload), apps/pii/redactor:rehydrate_text, apps/insights/markers:extract_and_record_insights, apps/router/charts:render_chart
inventory_notes: One typing indicator sent before the POST. Reasoning/BYO Claude models get REASONING_MODEL_TIMEOUT (240s) instead of DEFAULT_CHAT_TIMEOUT (120s). OpenRouter credit-limit (402 or credit-limit string in body) trips the budget circuit breaker and hibernates the container.

user_story: As the system, I want AI assistant markdown output to be converted to Telegram-safe HTML so that users see formatted text instead of raw markdown symbols.
expected_behaviour: render_telegram_html(text) parses CommonMark/GFM into blocks, renders each to Telegram HTML: headings→<b>, bold/italic/strikethrough→<b>/<i>/<s>, inline code→<code>, fenced blocks→<pre><code class="language-X">, links→<a href>, blockquotes→<blockquote>, lists→bullet/numbered/task-checkbox items, GFM tables→aligned <pre> monospace grid, --- rules→──────────. All literal text is HTML-escaped before tag insertion. Every block is validated by _is_valid_html against the allowed tag set; invalid blocks degrade to escaped plaintext. Output is split into ≤4096-char chunks on block boundaries. _send_telegram_markdown sends each chunk with parse_mode=HTML; on 400 rejection falls back to strip_telegram_html (tag-strip). Returns [] for empty input.
inventory_notes: Stdlib-only, no external dependencies. NUL bytes are stripped from input to prevent placeholder collision. The _is_valid_html validator checks tag balance and that only allowed tags appear; malformed content degrades to escaped plaintext, never raw markdown.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send photos to the bot and have them forwarded to my AI assistant so that the assistant can see and respond to images I share.
expected_behaviour: In the poller's _handle_update, if message has photo field, _upload_photo_to_workspace downloads the largest photo (max 5MB) from Telegram getFile, re-encodes as base64, stores to workspace/media/inbound/{hash}.{ext} on the tenant's Azure File Share via upload_workspace_file_binary, and returns the container-local path /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/media/inbound/.... The message_text is then prepended with [Photo attached: {path}]. On outbound, _send_photo (poller) and _send_telegram_photo (drain) download a workspace image from Azure File Share and call Telegram sendPhoto with multipart data. Captions are rendered via markdown_to_plaintext (max 1024 chars).
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (getFile, sendPhoto), Azure File Share (upload_workspace_file_binary, ShareFileClient)
inventory_notes: Photos >5MB are silently skipped (no download). The container-local path is what OpenClaw sees; the workspace mount maps it to the file share.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send voice messages to the bot and have them transcribed to text so that the AI assistant can understand and respond to spoken input.
expected_behaviour: In the poller's _extract_message_text, if message has voice or audio field, _transcribe_voice(file_id) is called. Downloads the audio via Telegram getFile + file download URL, then POSTs to OpenAI Whisper API (https://api.openai.com/v1/audio/transcriptions, model=whisper-1) with OPENAI_API_KEY. Returns transcribed text or None on failure. On success, message_text becomes '🎤 Voice message: "{transcript}"'. On failure: '[Voice message — couldn't transcribe, please try sending as text]'. Typing indicator is sent before transcription begins.
dependencies: OpenAI Whisper API (api.openai.com/v1/audio/transcriptions), Telegram Bot API (getFile, file download), OPENAI_API_KEY setting
inventory_notes: Returns None if OPENAI_API_KEY is not configured. File extension is inferred from the Telegram file_path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send text documents to the bot and have their content forwarded to my AI assistant so that it can process documents I share.
inventory_notes: No upload to file share — content is injected inline into the message text. Max content 10,000 chars.

feature: Sticker, video, location, contact message handling
user_story: As a subscriber, I want non-text Telegram messages (stickers, videos, locations, contacts) to be described to my AI assistant so that it can acknowledge and respond to them.
expected_behaviour: In _extract_message_text: sticker → '[User sent a sticker {emoji}]'; video/video_note → '[User sent a video ({duration}s, {size} MB)]' + optional caption; location (no venue) → '📍 User shared their location: {lat}, {lng} https://maps.google.com/maps?q={lat},{lng}'; venue → '📍 User shared a venue: {name} — {addr} ({lat},{lng}) {maps_url}'; contact → '📇 User shared a contact: {name} ({phone})'. Returns None for update types with no extractable text.
